_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve represents the relationship between Treasury yields across different maturities, typically from 1 month to 30 years. A normal yield curve slopes upward, indicating higher yields for longer maturities, reflecting expectations of growth and inflation. In 2023, the 10-year Treasury yield was below the 2-year yield, resulting in a deeply inverted curve with the 2s10s spread at -0.2 points, a historical signal often preceding recessions. Currently, the curve has re-steepened above zero, with the 2s10s spread at 0.28 points, indicating a modestly upward sloping curve, though still relatively flat.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread, the difference between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields, is a closely watched recession indicator because inversions have historically preceded every modern US recession by 6 to 18 months. The curve's previous inversion from 2022 through 2024 suggested elevated recession risk, but the current re-steepening above zero indicates the short-term signal effect may be transitioning. Complementing this, the NY Fed recession probability stands at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk but not certainty. Importantly, the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before the recession manifests, which provides nuance to using the 2s10s spread alone.
notes.now: At present, the yield curve’s modestly positive slope alongside a 31% Fed recession probability suggests that recession risks remain elevated but are not definitive. The un-inversion of the curve after a prolonged inversion signals some easing in short-term recession fears, while ongoing monitoring is warranted given the historical context.
